sourceTitle: Manage your inbox and calendar automatically with Custom Agents

questions.question: What is the main function of an Inbox Organizer Agent?

options.text: It automatically schedules meetings for you.
options.explanation: Scheduling meetings is handled by a Team Scheduler Agent, not the Inbox Organizer.

options.text: It triages, labels, drafts replies, and can import emails into Notion.
options.explanation: Correct – the Inbox Organizer sorts emails, drafts replies, and can create Notion entries.

options.text: It protects your calendar from overbooking.
options.explanation: Protecting calendar time is the role of a Calendar Optimizer Agent.

options.text: It syncs your email contacts with your Notion workspace.
options.explanation: Syncing contacts is not mentioned as a core function of the Inbox Organizer.

questions.question: Which label should you assign to newsletters and promotional emails according to the guide’s suggested labeling system?

options.text: 01. Reply
options.explanation: 01. Reply is for emails that need a response, not newsletters.

options.text: 03. Important notifications
options.explanation: Important notifications are for actionable items, not newsletters.

options.text: 05. Marketing
options.explanation: Correct – the guide maps newsletters/promotions to label 05. Marketing.

options.text: 02. Follow up
options.explanation: 02. Follow up is for items you’re waiting on, not marketing emails.

questions.question: When configuring a Calendar Optimizer Agent, which trigger timing is generally recommended to avoid noisy recommendations?

options.text: Run on every single Event updated.
options.explanation: Running on every tiny update creates excessive noise.

options.text: Run daily or scan ahead for the next few days.
options.explanation: Correct – batch scans (daily or multi‑day) keep suggestions helpful and less frequent.

options.text: Run only when you manually click a button.
options.explanation: Manual runs work but the guide emphasizes automated batch triggers.

options.text: Run whenever a free placeholder is added.
options.explanation: Free placeholders are not real commitments and should be ignored.

questions.question: Which filter would you apply to ensure a Calendar Optimizer Agent does not trigger on holidays or PTO?

options.text: Title contains "Holiday".
options.explanation: Title filter only catches specific words, not all all‑day events.

options.text: All‑day events: Exclude.
options.explanation: Correct – the guide lists excluding all‑day events to avoid triggering on PTO/holidays.

options.text: Participant RSVP status: Declined.
options.explanation: RSVP status filters by attendee response, not event type.

options.text: Busy status: Free.
options.explanation: Filtering on free status would ignore real commitments, not specifically holidays.

questions.question: Why is granting calendar access to a Team Scheduler Agent important for accurate meeting coordination?

options.text: It allows the agent to automatically send follow‑up emails.
options.explanation: Follow‑up emails are handled by the Inbox Organizer, not calendar access.

options.text: It enables the agent to see participants’ real availability and avoid double‑booking.
options.explanation: Correct – calendar access lets the agent read team calendars to find true free slots.

options.text: It lets the agent create tasks in your project tracker.
options.explanation: Task creation is a separate function; calendar access isn’t needed for that.

options.text: It provides the agent with email content for drafting replies.
options.explanation: Email content comes from mail access, not calendar access.

questions.question: What length of focus blocks does the Calendar Optimizer Agent prioritize when suggesting time for deep work?

options.text: 5–15 minutes
options.explanation: These are too short to be meaningful deep‑work blocks.

options.text: 30–120 minutes
options.explanation: Correct – the guide recommends focus blocks of 30–120 minutes.

options.text: 2–4 hours
options.explanation: While longer blocks can be useful, the guide specifically mentions 30–120 minutes.

options.text: Any free gap, regardless of length.
options.explanation: The agent filters out tiny gaps to keep suggestions practical.

questions.question: Which of the following is a recommended way to keep an Inbox Organizer Agent from drafting sensitive replies automatically?

options.text: Ask the agent to leave anything labeled 01. Reply as a draft for review.
options.explanation: Correct – the guide suggests leaving sensitive drafts for manual review.

options.text: Disable the Draft replies step entirely.
options.explanation: Disabling the step removes useful automation; the guide recommends selective drafting.

options.text: Set the trigger to run only when a new email arrives.
options.explanation: Trigger timing doesn’t control sensitivity handling.

options.text: Label sensitive emails as 05. Marketing.
options.explanation: Marketing label is for newsletters, not for sensitive messages.

questions.question: How does stacking multiple Custom Agents enhance productivity according to the guide?

options.text: It allows one agent to replace the others, reducing overall complexity.
options.explanation: Stacking combines agents, not replaces them.

options.text: Each agent handles a specific part of a workflow, and together they automate end‑to‑end processes.
options.explanation: Correct – the guide illustrates how agents work together to manage inbox, schedule, and calendar optimization.

options.text: It creates duplicate actions, ensuring no step is missed.
options.explanation: Duplicate actions could cause conflicts; stacking is meant to coordinate, not duplicate.

options.text: It reduces the need for any manual triggers, making the system fully autonomous.
options.explanation: Manual triggers may still be needed for oversight; the guide doesn’t claim full autonomy.

questions.question: Which filter would you use if you only want a Calendar Optimizer Agent to run for meetings titled "1:1" or "Customer"?

options.text: Organizer email contains "1:1".
options.explanation: Organizer email filter looks at email addresses, not titles.

options.text: Title contains "1:1" or "Customer".
options.explanation: Correct – the title filter can limit triggers to specific meeting types.

options.text: Participant RSVP status is "Accepted".
options.explanation: RSVP status filters on attendance, not on meeting titles.

options.text: Busy status is "Free".
options.explanation: Busy status filter is unrelated to meeting naming.

questions.question: What is a key benefit of setting the Inbox Organizer Agent to run on a schedule rather than on every new email arrival?

options.text: It ensures every email gets an immediate draft reply.
options.explanation: Immediate replies are a benefit of real‑time triggers, not scheduled runs.

options.text: It gives you a clean, organized inbox at the start of each day.
options.explanation: Correct – the guide recommends scheduled runs for a tidy morning inbox.

options.text: It prevents the agent from ever drafting replies.
options.explanation: Drafting can still occur; schedule just changes timing.

options.text: It disables the ability to import emails into Notion.
options.explanation: Import functionality is independent of trigger timing.
